coredata.dc:title: Influence of Zn2+ doping on the lattice defects and photoluminescence studies of Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ nanophosphor: Applications for data encryption strategies
coredata.dc:creator: Sudheendra, H.S.
Darshan, G.P.
Basavaraj, R.B.
Naik, Yashwanth V.
Premakumar, H.B.
Nagabhushana, H.
Williams, J.F.
Hareesh, K.
Kokila, M.K.
coredata.dc:description: In the present work, luminescent Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %), Zn2+ (0.25–3 mol %) nanophosphors were fabricated by the ultrasound irradiated sonochemical method. The samples prepared with 3 h ultrasound treatment exhibit well crystalline and single phase Sr2CeO4. The dumbbell shaped morphology of the prepared samples was elucidated from both SEM and TEM results. The energy band gap of the prepared samples was estimated and found to be in the range ∼3.18–3.63 eV. The incorporation of Zn2+ greatly influences the defect and emission intensities, as revealed from photoluminescence and positron lifetime spectroscopy measurements. The enhancement in the photoluminescence emission intensity after Zn2+ incorporation was observed, which may be due to the creation of defects and efficient energy transfer between Zn2+ and Eu3+ or defects that can act as emission centers. The positron lifetime spectroscopy qualitatively explains the concentration of defects (vacancy and voids) which are induced by Zn2+ co-doping. The encryption strategies are provided using the photoluminescent chalk for high level information protection. We believe that the versatile, convenient and user-friendly strategy demonstrated herein will open a new insight for on-site information protection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultrasonication route
Positron annihilation spectroscopy
Doppler broadening spectroscopy
Photoluminescence
Luminescent chalk

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication and characterization of the PL chalk

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of formation of Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %), Zn2+ (0.25–3 mol %) nanophosphor by ultrasound assisted sonochemical route.
Schematic view for the fabrication of luminescent chalk prepared using Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %), Zn2+ (3 mol %) nanophosphor.
(a) PXRD profiles, (b) W-H plots, (c) DR spectra and (d) Energy band gap plot of prepared samples.
SEM images of (a) Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %) and (b–f) Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %): Zn2+ (0.25–3 mol %) nanophosphors, respectively.
(a–e) TEM images of Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %): Zn2+ (0.25–3 mol %) nanophosphors and (f) HRTEM image of optimized sample.

(a) PL excitation spectrum, (b) PL emission spectra of Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (1–11 mol %), (c & d) PLE spectra of Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %): Zn2+ (0.25–3 mol %) nanophosphors excited at 300 and 396 nm, respectively and (e) energy level diagram of Eu3+ ions in the Sr2CeO4 host.

(a) Variation of PL intensity as a function of Zn2+ concentration, (b) Logarithmic plot of (x) versus (I/x), (c & d) CIE and CCT diagram of Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %): Zn2+ (0.25–3 mol %) nanophosphors, respectively.
PL decay profiles of (a) Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %) and (b–d) Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %): Zn2+ (0.25, 1, 3 mol %) nanophosphors respectively.

(a) Peak-normalized PLS of Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %) and Zn2+ co-doped (0.25–3 mol %) nanophosphor, (b) The positron lifetimes (τ
1
& τ
2
) parameters as a function of Zn2+ concentration and (c) Variation of lifetime intensities (I1 and I2) with co-dopant concentration.
(a) Linear fitted diagram of S v/s W parameters and (b) Variations of S and W parameters with Zn2+ concentration.
Schematic illustration and revealed results of luminescent chalk prepared by using optimized nanophosphor on various surfaces.
Estimated average crystallite size, micro-strain and energy band gap of Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %) and Zn2+ co-doped Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ nanophosphors.
Photometric characteristics of Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %) and Sr2CeO4:Eu3+, Zn2+ (0.25–3 mol %) nanophosphors.
PAS lifetime parameters, average lifetime and CDBS (S & W) parameters of Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %) and Sr2CeO4:Eu3+, Zn2+ (0.25–3 mol %) nanophosphors.

head.ce:title: Influence of Zn2+ doping on the lattice defects and photoluminescence studies of Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ nanophosphor: Applications for data encryption strategies

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Govt. Science College, Bangalore, 560 001, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Bangalore University, Bangalore, 560 056, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Acharya Institute of Graduate Studies, Bengaluru, 560 107, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Prof. C.N.R. Rao Centre for Advanced Materials, Tumkur University, Tumkur, 572 103, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Prof. C.N.R. Rao Centre for Advanced Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Ramaiah University of Applied Sciences, Bangalore, 560 054, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: f
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Antimatter Matter Studies, Centre for Atomic, Molecular and Surface Physics, School of Physics, University of Western Australia, Perth, WA, 6009, Australia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: g
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Applied Sciences (Physics), REVA University, Bangalore, 560 064, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present work, luminescent Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %), Zn2+ (0.25–3 mol %) nanophosphors were fabricated by the ultrasound irradiated sonochemical method. The samples prepared with 3 h ultrasound treatment exhibit well crystalline and single phase Sr2CeO4. The dumbbell shaped morphology of the prepared samples was elucidated from both SEM and TEM results. The energy band gap of the prepared samples was estimated and found to be in the range ∼3.18–3.63 eV. The incorporation of Zn2+ greatly influences the defect and emission intensities, as revealed from photoluminescence and positron lifetime spectroscopy measurements. The enhancement in the photoluminescence emission intensity after Zn2+ incorporation was observed, which may be due to the creation of defects and efficient energy transfer between Zn2+ and Eu3+ or defects that can act as emission centers. The positron lifetime spectroscopy qualitatively explains the concentration of defects (vacancy and voids) which are induced by Zn2+ co-doping. The encryption strategies are provided using the photoluminescent chalk for high level information protection. We believe that the versatile, convenient and user-friendly strategy demonstrated herein will open a new insight for on-site information protection.

ce:section.ce:para: The surface effects are more dominant in nanoparticles as compared its bulk counterparts owing to its large surface to volume ratio. As a result, these nanoparticles showed extraordinary electronic and optical properties. Further, the existence of impurities, such as vacancy, vacancy cluster at the grain and grain boundaries can affect greatly the materials performance. These defects enhance the material properties. Hence, the defects related studies are highly required for material characterization [1,2].

In recent years, Positron lifetime annihilation spectroscopy (PALS) is one of the sensitive tools to analyze the unique surface defects in nanoparticles due to the prolonged diffusion length of positrons compared to the size of the nanoparticles [3]. When material is implanted with positrons, after thermalisation positrons diffuses in the material followed by annihilation with an electron. Due to lower electron density, the defect induced by the positrons may lead to longer lifetime [4]. Therefore, the size and concentration of defects directly correlated to positron lifetimes. However, the electron momentum distribution can be obtained by Coincidence Doppler Broadening (CDB) measurements. CDB studies, a comparatively innovative technique, comprises coincidence measurement of two 511 keV annihilation photons at 180 °C that expands the peak to background ratio and enables the explicit extraction of the shape as well as the magnitude of the core electron component of the Doppler spectrum [5]. In addition, luminescence properties are highly influenced by the existence of lattice defects, namely; mono vacancy, vacancy cluster, voids or pores, etc. which are commonly occurring during material preparation. Lu et al. reported that the PLE greatly affects the lattice defect created in Eu3+/Dy3+ co-doped SrAl2Si2O8/SrAlSi1/2O7/2 phosphor by replacing Sr by Eu and Dy [6]. Further, the charge imbalance between La3+ and Ca2+ in CaAl2O4 phosphor induces the defects in the material [7]. Thorat et al. investigated that the formation of vacancy clusters within the nanoparticles in CeO2: Eu, as a result PLE intensity can be enhanced [8].

The design strategy to protect the security and integrity of information involves three components: materials, encryption and decryption techniques. The materials are used to store graphical or textual information and is desirable that (i) they are biocompatible to exclude the potential toxicity and harmful effects for users [9], (ii) they are fabricated easily at low-cost and available in large scale [10], and (iii) they have superior physical and chemical stability to prevent non-artificial destruction such as photo-bleaching of organic dyes [11]. As for encryption techniques, they should make the hidden information hard to be recognized under normal conditions [12], whereas, for the decryption techniques, it should be convenient and rapid for the original data to be acquired and interpreted with portable and inexpensive equipment [13]. Among the various strategies used for information protection in the previous reports, security inks made from Photoluminescent (PL) materials are the most widely studied, including those show down- and up-conversion photoluminescence and phosphorescence [14–17]. The particular attractive features of the security ink are that (i) PL materials can be synthesized in green and facile ways, making low-cost and mass production possible [18,19], (ii) security inks are highly compatible with printing techniques such as screen and inkjet printing [20–25], which are flexible in operation and patterns design for large-area data encryption with high resolution, and (iii) the data decryption, that is readout of the hidden information printed with security inks, can be achieved using portable devices such as mobile phone or ultraviolet (UV) light [26,27].

For the past decades, significant research was performed to explore novel inorganic luminescent nanomaterials [28]. Among them, blue emission Sr2CeO4 luminescent material was found to be highly efficient owing to its broadband emission spectrum as well as energy can significantly transfer to dopant ions, as a result, it can be effectively used as a temperature sensor [29], a source of anti-Stokes broadband white light [30], X-ray detectors [31], blue phosphors in the lightening industry or field emission displays [32]. In the twentieth century, Sr2CeO4 was successfully synthesized by Danielson et al. [33] by using combinatorial method. The obtained results reveal one-dimensional chains of edge sharing CeO6 octahedral, with two terminal oxygen atoms per cerium center, which was isolated from one another by strontium cations. At present, several routes were well documented for the fabrication of Sr2CeO4, namely high-temperature solid state method [34], combustion synthesis [35], sol–gel method [36], etc. These conventional routes required high temperatures or prolonged reaction time.

The basic phenomena involved in ultrasonication process are cavitation (i.e., bubbles). The cavitation process involves the formation, growth and collapse of micro bubbles. The ultrasonic irradiation will induce bubble formation in the precursor mixture. The generated bubbles will grow due to successive expansion and depression of ultrasound. Then, the bubbles over grow up to a particular stage, then leads to violent collapsing. This explosion provides extreme conditions (i.e., temperature > 5000 K and pressure >500 bar) in a liquid, results in well crystalline nanostructures [37].

To the best of our knowledge, the positron annihilation studies on Zn2+ co-doped Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ nanophosphors are not explored elsewhere. Therefore, we reported Zn2+ co-doped Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ nanophosphors are synthesized by sonochemical route. The structural, PL and photometric studies are extensively investigated. Further, we have employed a highly sensitive PALS and CDBS tools to study the defects in Zn2+ co-doped Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ nanophosphors. In addition, the optimized nanophosphor was used as a novel material for data encryption.

To prepare Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %), Zn2+ (0.25–3 mol %) nanophosphor, the stoichiometric quantities of Cerium (III) nitrate hexahydrate [Ce(NO3)3·6H2O:99.99%, Sigma Aldrich], strontium nitrate [Sr(NO3)2: 99%, Sigma Aldrich], Europium (III) nitrate pentahydrate [Eu(NO3)3·5H2O: 99.9%, Sigma Aldrich] and Zinc nitrate hexahydrate [Zn(NO3)2·6H2O: 98%, Sigma Aldrich] were taken and well dissolved in deionized water. The reactants were thoroughly mixed using a magnetic stirrer. Further, the pH of the solution was maintained by adding NaOH (0.1 mol %). The precursor solution was sonicated by using probe sonicator (frequency: 20 kHz; power: 300 W) for ∼ 180 min. The obtained precipitate was filtered and washed several times with double distilled water and alcohol. The resultant powder was dried in a hot air oven at ∼80 °C for 12 h and then heat treated at ∼700 °C for 3 h. Fig. 1 shows the schematic illustration for the sonochemical synthesis of Zn2+ co-doped Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ nanophosphor.

The 500 mg of the optimized Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %), Zn2+ (3 mol %) nanophosphor was mixed with ethanol (2 ml) which was then subjected to grinding gently. The resultant powder was palletized in the form of chalk using a cylindrical metal mold. The obtained chalk was dried in an oven maintained at ∼70 °C subsequently calcined to ∼700 °C. The schematic view for the fabrication of luminescent chalk was shown in Fig. 2 .

The structural studies were made using X ray diffractometer (Shimadzu −7000) provided with Cu-Kα radiation and nickel filter (k = 0.15406 nm). Scanning electron microscope (Hitachi- TM 3000) and Transmission electron microscope (Hitachi H-8100, Kevex sigma TM Quasar, USA) were used to study the morphology as well as the size of the particles, respectively. The diffuse reflectance (DR) studies were performed by using a Perkin Elmer Spectrophotometer (Lambda – 35). The PLE studies were done using Jobin Yvon made Fluorolog-3 Spectrofluorimeter. The PALS measurements were recorded using a fast-fast coincidence spectrometer provided with 30 μCi22 NaCl source, BC418 scintillators detectors and Burle 8850 photomultiplier tubes (Burle Industries, Inc., Lancaster, PA). Two germanium detectors with different energy resolutions (1.27 keV and 1.33 keV) were utilized to measure the CDB. The obtained data were well analyzed with help of PALSfit 3.0 software.

PXRD profiles of Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %), Zn2+ (0.25–3 mol %) nanophosphor was shown in Fig. 3 (a). As can be seen from the profiles, samples prepared with 1 and 2 h ultrasonication exhibit mixed phases (Sr2CeO4, SrCeO3, CeO2 and SrCO3). However, with the increase of ultrasound treatment for 3 h, single phased Sr2CeO4 can be achieved and profiles were well matched with standard JCPDS.No:50–0115 [38]. No obvious changes in the diffraction profiles were observed, suggesting that the dopant (Eu3+) and co-dopant (Zn2+) ions were effectively substituted in the host Sr2CeO4 sites. The effective ionic radii of Eu3+ (1.09 Å for CN = 6), Ce4+ (1.01 Å for CN = 6), Sr2+ (1.32 Å for CN = 6), O2− (1.26 Å for CN = 6) and Zn2+ (0.88 Å for CN = 6). Hence, it is obvious that Zn2+ ions can substitute for the Sr2+ site. The average crystallite size (D) and strain (ε) of the prepared samples were estimated by using both the Scherrer's and WH plot method [39,40];
(1)
D =
0.9 λ
β
cos
θ
(2)
β
cos
θ
λ
=
1
D
+
ε
sin
θ
λ
where λ; wavelength of the X-ray radiation, β; full width at half maximum (FWHM) of PXRD peaks, ε; total strain present in the samples. The WH plots of Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %), Zn2+ (0.25–3 mol %) nanophosphor were depicted in Fig. 3 (b). The “ε” and “D” values are estimated from the slope and intercept of the straight line in the WH plots, respectively. The estimated values were summarized in Table 1 .

Fig. 3 (c) depicts the DR spectra of Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %) and Zn2+ co-doped Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %) nanophosphors. The spectra exhibit absorption bands at ∼360, 394, 466 and 572 nm, which were attributed to 7F0→5D4, 7F0→5L6, 7F0→5D2, 7F0→5D0 transitions of Eu3+ ions, respectively [41]. The Kubelka–Munk (K-M) theory was utilized to calculate the energy band gap (Eg) of the prepared nanophosphor. The K–M function F (R∞) and photon energy (
h ν
) was calculated using following relations [42];
(3)
F
(
R
∞
)
=
(
1 −
R
∞
)
2
2
R
∞
(4)
h ν =
1240
λ
where R∞; reflection coefficient of the sample, λ; absorption wavelength. From the plots of
[
F
(
R
∞
)
]
2
as a function of photon energy (
h ν
) (Fig. 3 (d)), the Eg values were estimated by extrapolating the linear fitted regions to
[
F
(
R
∞
)
]
2
= 0. The Eg values of the prepared nanophosphors were calculated and listed in Table 1. The noticeable disparity in the Eg values were manipulated by structural changes.

Fig. 4 shows the SEM micrographs of Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %) and Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %): Zn2+ (0.25–3 mol %) nanophosphors. The dumbbell shaped particles with uneven size was observed in Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %) nanophosphors (Fig. 4 (a)). Upon Zn2+ (0.25–3 mol %) incorporation, the agglomeration increases and dumbbell shaped particles were completely disappeared as shown in Fig. 4(b–f). Further, TEM images of Eu3+ (5 mol %) doped and Zn2+ co-doped Sr2CeO4 nanophosphors was depicted Fig. 5 (a–e). The similar dumbbell shaped particles with agglomeration were clearly observed. The obtained results were better agreed with SEM images. Further, the interplanar distance (d) of Zn2+ (3 mol %) doped sample was estimated and found to be ∼0.25 nm (Fig. 5 (f)).

Fig. 6 (a) shows the PL excitation spectrum of Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %) nanophosphor recorded in the range of 200–450 nm. The spectrum consists of a broad charge transfer (CT) band ranging from 200 to 400 nm with maximum at ∼ 300 nm, which was assigned to the charge transfer process of Ce4+→O2− [43].

The broadband was deconvoluted resulting in peaks at ∼275, 293 and 327 nm. The first band, at 275 nm, is assigned to the charge transfer process Eu3+→O2− [44]. The bands at 293 and 327 nm are related to charge transfer between Ce4+ and O2− on the Sr2CeO4 structure. The band with maximum at 293 nm is associated with the axial transfer O2− → Ce4+ in the octahedral structure (CeO6) and the shoulder at 327 nm is associated with the equatorial transfer O2− → Ce4+ [43]. One band at 300 nm is observed, which is assigned with direct absorption of Eu3+ [45].

The PL emission spectra of Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (1–11 mol %) nanophosphor upon 300 nm excitation was shown in Fig. 6 (b). A broad emission spectrum with the peak at 475 nm originating from the Ce4+–O2- CTS transition can be observed, as well as the characteristic emission of Eu3+ ions. The weak peaks related with the characteristic emission of Eu3+ ions are located at 467, 490, 534, 555, 585, 612 and 704 nm, which are attributed to 5D2→7F0, 5D2→7F2, 5D1→7F1, 5D1→7F2, 5D0→7F1, 5D0→7F2 and 5D0→7F4 transitions, respectively [45].

Further, the decrement in intensity of CeO CT band and enhancement of luminescence from Eu3+ (5D0→7F2) transition was observed with increase of Eu3+ concentration (Fig. 6 (b)). This can be due to the creation of oxygen vacancies seems to be the dominant incorporation mechanism. As a result, these oxygen vacancies, which can act as sensitizers [46], facilitate the strong mixing of the CeO and EuO charge transfer states, and thus promote energy migration from the CeO CT band to Eu3+. Furthermore, the more oxygen vacancies generated by doping ions, the more effective energy transfer happens between Ce4+ and Eu3+ ions. The luminescence quenching of the CeO CT band, it is not only for the smaller wave function overlap between the holes on the ligands O− and the electrons on the Ce3+ ions but also because of promotion of energy transfer from the CeO CT band to Eu3+ ions.

Fig. 6 (c & d) shows the emission spectra of Zn2+ (0.25–3 mol %) co-doped Sr2CeO4: Eu3+ (5 mol %) nanophosphor excited at 300 and 396 nm wavelengths, respectively. As evident from the emission spectra, there is no change in the peak positions of the Eu3+ emission with the addition of Zn2+ co-doping. However, a significant enhancement in the PL emission intensities as relative with Eu3+ (5 mol %) were observed. This result indicates that, the nanophosphor with a suitable concentration of Zn2+ can enhance the PL intensity. Different mechanisms available in the literature to explain the PL intensity enhancement with Zn2+ incorporation are as follows. (i) creation of defects during charge compensation which acts as a luminescence center (ii) some efficient energy transfer may exist between Zn2+ and Eu3+ ions that improves the luminous intensity of Eu3+ ions [47] and (iii) ZnO may act as the role of solid flux in the experiment and stimulate the host lattice formation and grain growth. The absorption and emission transitions in the Sr2CeO4: Eu3+ nanophosphor occur due to the CeO CT band transitions as illustrated in the energy diagram shown in Fig. 6 (e). It can be seen that energy transfer process starts from the electron pumping from ground state to the allowed low spin singlet excited state (LS) of Sr2CeO4 nanophosphor [48,49]. Subsequently, the electron is transferred to close lying charge transfer Eu3+→ O2− transition through the dipole–dipole interaction. After non-radiative relaxation to emitting levels of Eu3+ ion the luminescence form 5Dj (j = 0, 1, 2) levels.

The variation of PL intensity with Zn2+ concentration was depicted in Fig. 7 (a). It was evident that the gradual increase of PL intensity with increase of the Zn2+ concentration was clearly noticed. According to Dexter and Schulman, the strength or type of the multipolar interaction among dopants was estimated by using the following relations [50];
(5)
I
x
= k
[
1 + β
(
x
)
Q / 3
]
− 1
where x; activator concentration, Q; exchange interaction, where Q = 6, 8 or 10 for electric dipole-dipole (D-D), dipole-quadrupole (D-Q), or quadrupole-quadrupole (Q-Q) interactions, respectively. K and β are the constants for a given host lattice. The logarithmic plot of (I/x) versus (x) for Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %): Zn2+ (0.25–3 mol %) nanophosphor was shown in Fig. 7 (b). The value of the slope and Q was found to be −0.55 and 6.94, respectively. The obtained value of Q was close to 6. This indicates that d–d interaction was the main responsible for the energy transfer mechanism in the present sample.

The CIE diagram and its color co-ordinates of Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %) and Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %): Zn2+ (0.25–3 mol %) nanophosphors were depicted in Fig. 7 (c). The CIE chromaticity co-ordinates of Sr2CeO4: Eu3+ (5 mol %) nanophosphor were located in the orange red region. However, the remarkable shift in the emission color from orange to deep red region was clearly noticed in co-doped samples. The color tuning by appropriate doping and co-doping is very important property and hence it can be utilized for many technological applications. Further, the correlated color temperature (CCT) was calculated by using the following equations [51];
(6)
u ′
=
4 x
− 2 x
+
12 y

(7)

=
9 y
− 2 x

+ 3
(8)
C C T
( x ,
y )
=
− 449
n
3
+ 3525
n
2
−
6823.3 n
+ 5520.33
where, n = (x – 0.3320)/(y – 0.1858). Fig. 7 (d) shows the CCT diagram of the prepared nanophosphors. The average CCT values of prepared samples were found to be ∼2643 K. Further, the color purity is a major tool in order to assess the significance of the nanophosphors. Hence, the color purity of prepared nanophosphors was estimated by using the following equation [52];
(9)
c o l o r
p u r i t y =
(

where, (x

d
, y
d ); color co-ordinates of the light source, CIE illuminant and dominant wavelength of light source, respectively. In the present case, the color purity of the prepared samples was estimated and listed in Table 2 . The color purity of the optimized sample was found to be ∼72%. Hence, the optimized nanophosphor finds potential application in the production of WLEDs as an orange-red phosphor.

To investigate the energy migration process, the luminescence decay profiles of Eu3+ ions were studied. Fig. 8 (a–d) displays PL decay profiles of Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %) and Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %), Zn2+ (0.25, 1 and 3 mol %) nanophosphors excited at 396 nm and monitored at 612 nm together with the corresponding fitting exponential lines. Generally, when the distance between activators drops when their concentrations increases. Consequently, the energy transfer process among activators comes into effect significantly, which acts as an extra luminescence decay approach. Thus, the decay time could be affected by the energy migration process between activators in the host [53].

The decay profiles of Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %) and Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %), Zn2+ (0.25, 1 and 3 mol %) NPs can be best fitted using the double exponential formula;
(10)
I =
A
1
exp
( − t /
τ
1
)
+
A
2
exp
( − t /
τ
2
)
where A1 and A2 represent constants, I is the luminescence intensity, t is the time, and τ1 and τ2 are decay constants of exponential aspects. The double exponential decay curves indicate the possible interactions between Eu3+ ions. The average lifetime (τavg) of Sr2CeO4:Eu3+, Zn2+ nanophosphors were calculated by using a following equation [54];
(11)
τ
a v e r a g e
=
I
1
τ
1
2
+
I
2
τ
2
2
I
1
τ
1
+
I
2
τ
2
The estimated lifetime values were found to be 12.32 ms for Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %) and 14.62, 15.96 and 17.21 ms for Zn2+ ions co-doped (0.25, 1, 3 mol%) samples.

The peak-normalized PLS of Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ and Zn2+ co-doped (0.25–3 mol %) nanophosphors was depicted in Fig. 9 (a). The spectra comprise into two components (increase and decrease counts). The increase component is due to the occurrence of positive ions, while the decreased component arises due to the creation of extra vacancies in the nanophosphors. The PALSfit program was utilized to resolve the PLS into two lifetime components with respect to their intensities [55]. The lifetime parameters (τ
1
& τ
2
) and their corresponding intensities as a function of Zn2+ concentration were shown in Fig. 9 (b & c). The estimated lifetime parameters are given in Table 3 . It was found that, one shorter (τ
1
) and other longer lifetimes (τ
2
) in Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ nanophosphors. Among them, τ 1 (0.181 ns) with intensity (I1) of ∼92.4% is much lesser than the τ 2 value (0.86 ns) with intensity ∼ 6.6%. Higher τ 2 value is due to free positrons and trapped positrons at point defects and extended defects [56]. Therefore, the higher τ 2 is owing to the bulk vacancy type defect present in Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %) nanophosphors. However, the lower lifetime (τ 1) was due to the positron trapped at the cluster vacancy defects in the crystallites and grain boundary [57]. Upon Zn2+ ions incorporation, the size of the vacancy type defect as well as cluster vacancies gets reduced. These vacancies decrease I1 (from 92.4 to 82.5%) and increase I2 (from 6.6 to 17.07%) values. The gradual decrease of lifetimes (τ 1 and τ 2) relative to Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %) nanophosphor was due to the grain boundary defects in Zn doped Sr2CeO4. The significant reduction of crystallize size after Zn2+ incorporation enhances the concentration of defects in the grain boundary. Therefore, the concentration of cluster vacancy defects, micro-void and their size increases in the grain boundary. Further, the average positron lifetime value (τ
avg ) is calculated using the formula [58];
(12)
τ
a v g
=
∑
τ
i
I
i
∑
I
i
where τi and Ii; lifetime and intensity of positron. The values of τ
av were estimated and listed in Table 3. The decrement in the τavg was observed after co-doping, which may due creation of defects induced by charge imbalance and ionic radii mismatch between Sr2+ and Zn2+ ion.

Fig. 10 (a) depicts the linear fit between SW parameters exhibiting that the defect clusters, as well as trap positrons relatively the same origin and configuration. The S and W parameters in the low and the high momentum region are estimated using Doppler broadening in order to investigate the defects in details [59]. The S
W parameters for Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %) and Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %), Zn2+ (0.25–3 mol %) nanophosphor as shown in Fig. 10 (b). The gradual increase of S parameter and corresponding decrease in W parameter was observed after positron trapping (Fig. 10 (b)). The increment of the S parameter in the Zn2+ incorporated samples clearly confirms the creation of more vacancies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of the PL chalk
ce:section.ce:para: Till date many materials were used to manufacture of PL chalk, including organic dyes [60], quantum dots [61], metal-organic framework [62], and Ln-based materials [63]. Among them, Zn2+ co-doped Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %) nanophosphor an ideal material due to several advantages over others, such as pure fluorescent color, sharp emission, low toxicity, low cost, high stability, high quantum yield, and high synthesis yield. In addition, Zn2+ co-doped Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %) nanophosphor based PL chalk will not produce negative impact on human beings and the environment and provides necessary mechanical stability for the practical usage.

The PL chalks with similar white color and shape to the commercial chalk were shown in Fig. 11 . Under irradiation with a UV light (254 nm), the PL chalks made from optimized Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %), Zn2+ (3 mol %) nanophosphor exhibited brilliant red fluorescence. It was noteworthy that the prepared chalks are portable and writable. These portable and writable behaviors of the PL chalk made the information protection possible at anytime and anyplace, offering advantages over the previously reported strategies. Further, the PL chalk fabricated using optimized sample exhibit high compatibility with the various substrates. Schematic illustration of luminescent chalk on various surfaces was shown in Fig. 11 (a). We directly wrote the English letters on the arbitrary substrate including metal, wood, leaf, ceramic crucible, glass and granite surface (Fig. 11 (b)). Upon exposure to UV light, the information on the substrates was clearly visible with naked eyes and showed strong red fluorescence. The white color PL chalk manufactured by using Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %), Zn2+ (3 mol %) nanophosphor has added advantage due to more suitability for writing on the substrates with light background, such as paper, metal, fabric, plastic, wall, foam and human hands. In these cases, the as-written information was almost invisible, preventing information from visual observation and thus making it harder for recognition.

In summary, highly luminescent Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %), Zn2+ (0.25–3 mol %) nanophosphors were successfully prepared by sonochemical route. A single phased Sr2CeO4 can be achieved and profiles were well matched with standard JCPDS.NO:50–0115 with an increase of ultrasound treatment for 3 h. The dumbbell shaped particles with agglomeration was observed in both SEM and TEM results. The PL intensity decrement and enhancement of CeO CT band and Eu3+ transitions with increases of Eu3+ concentration was due to the creation of oxygen vacancies. The CIE color coordinates tuned from orange to intense red after Zn2+ co-doped with Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ samples. PALS confirmed that, upon Zn2+ ions incorporation, the size of the vacancy type defect as well as cluster vacancies gets reduced. These vacancies decrease I1 (from 92.4 to 82.5%) and increase I2 (from 6.6 to 17.07%). The gradual decrease of lifetimes (τ 1 and τ 2) in Zn2+ co-doped Sr2CeO4 nanophosphor relative to Sr2CeO4:Eu3+ (5 mol %) nanophosphor was due to the grain boundary defects. The decrement in the τavg was observed after co-doping, which may also due creation of defects induced by charge imbalance and ionic radii mismatch between Sr2+ and Zn2+ ion. The presence of defects is quantitatively and qualitatively explained using PALS studies. In addition, we also provide some strategies for data encryption using PL chalk. High-level information protection was designed based on the proposed encryption strategies. Encouraged by these results, it was believed that the as-demonstrated portable and writable PL chalk points out a new direction for the development of information protection.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:
Yes, the authors have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
